name: Begonia chlorosticta
aboutText: Begonia chlorosticta, described by Sands, is a rare Bornean rhizomatous species from the rainforest understory of Sarawak — growing in deep shade on moist, well-drained humus-rich substrate among leaf litter and decaying organic matter, with the high humidity and stable warmth of a tropical forest environment maintained year-round. The leaves are the plant's entire spectacle: asymmetrical, ovate to heart-ovate blades of dark foliage patterned with luminous lime-green spots of considerable intensity, subtly bullate in texture, borne on reddish petioles with deep red undersides. Growth proceeds from a creeping rhizome at substrate level, forming compact clumps. Among the spotted begonias of Borneo, chlorosticta occupies a position at the very top of the collector hierarchy for the sheer contrast between its near-black blade and the vivid chartreuse spotting.

quickFacts.light: Low to medium indirect light (deep shade tolerant)

morphology.leafShape: Asymmetrical, ovate to heart-ovate, subtly bullate
morphology.texture: Subtly bullate, semi-matte, deep red undersides
morphology.variegation: Luminous lime-green spots on near-black blade (natural species pattern)
morphology.growthHabit: Compact creeping rhizome at substrate surface

propagation.notes: Most rhizomatous begonias root readily from leaf sections or petiole cuttings placed on moist substrate. Keep warm and humid until plantlets emerge at leaf margins.

careGuide.substrate: Light, well-draining mix: 50% perlite, 30% potting compost, 20% orchid bark. Begonias are susceptible to root rot — excellent drainage is essential.
careGuide.watering: Allow the top half of the substrate to dry between waterings. Water at the base — wet leaves invite fungal problems. Reduce in winter.
careGuide.humidity: 50–70%. More tolerant of average indoor humidity than tropical aroids, but consistent moisture prevents crispy leaf margins.
careGuide.fertilising: Balanced liquid fertiliser at quarter strength every 2–3 weeks during the growing season. Do not feed in winter.
careGuide.repotting: Every 12–18 months in spring. Begonias like shallow, wide pots that suit rhizomatous growth. Don't bury the rhizome.

commonProblems.cause: Poor air circulation combined with high humidity
commonProblems.fix: Improve airflow; avoid wetting leaves; treat with a dilute bicarbonate of soda spray

commonProblems.cause: Overwatering, dead leaves left on plant, poor air circulation
commonProblems.fix: Remove dead foliage promptly; increase airflow; reduce watering
Move to a brighter position — begonias need good indirect light to maintain compact growth

items: Check for powdery mildew — a white dusty coating on leaf surfaces, especially in dry conditions; it spreads rapidly between plants
Inspect for mealybugs at leaf axils and the underside of stems where they hide in white cottony deposits
Rhizome begonias should show firm, healthy rhizome tissue — avoid plants with soft, discoloured rhizomes
Most begonias ship well as leaf cuttings — if buying a cutting, ensure it has a healthy petiole and no signs of rot at the cut end

fieldNotes.title: Pale Dots on Dark Ground
fieldNotes.author: Aroid Aaron
fieldNotes.content: A Sarawak begonia with leaves covered in scattered pale yellow or cream dots — the name chlorosticta means 'green spot,' which is somewhat misleading given the spots are paler than the ground colour, but taxonomy is not always a descriptive science. The spotting pattern is quite unlike the bold polka-dot arrangement of Begonia maculata and is subtler, more fine and scattered, which suits certain aesthetic sensibilities better. Genuinely uncommon in cultivation. Humid terrarium conditions required. The kind of plant you find in someone's collection and spend five minutes trying to identify.
